sections.title: Processes & jobs
sections.subtitle: See, control and manage everything running on your system

lessons.title: What is a process?
lessons.description: Understand processes and list what's running with ps

steps.label: Processes and PIDs
steps.text:
Every program running on Linux is a <hl>process</hl>. Each gets a unique <hl>PID</hl> (Process ID).

<cmd>ps</cmd>          — show your own processes
<cmd>ps aux</cmd>      — show ALL processes from ALL users
<cmd>ps aux | grep nginx</cmd> — find a specific process

Key columns in ps aux:
<hl>PID</hl>   — process ID
<hl>%CPU</hl>  — CPU usage
<hl>%MEM</hl>  — memory usage
<hl>STAT</hl>  — status (R=running, S=sleeping, Z=zombie)
<hl>COMMAND</hl> — the program name

terminal.output:
USER       PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root         1  0.0  0.1  22560  1024 ?        Ss   09:00   0:01 /sbin/init
user      1234  0.2  0.8  45320  8192 pts/0    S    09:05   0:00 bash
user      1290  0.0  0.1  17820  1024 pts/0    R+   09:10   0:00 ps aux

feedback.ok: Correct! PID is the unique Process ID assigned to every running program.
feedback.bad: PID stands for Process ID — a unique number Linux assigns to each running process.

steps.label: Reading ps output
steps.question: In ps aux, a process shows STAT as 'Z'. What does this mean?
options: It is a zombie process — finished but not cleaned up
It is paused by the user
It is running at maximum speed

feedback.ok: Correct! Z = zombie. The process finished but its parent hasn't acknowledged it yet.
feedback.bad: Z in the STAT column means zombie — the process has exited but hasn't been fully cleaned up by its parent.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To find all running nginx processes, combine ps aux with a

feedback.ok: Correct! ps aux | grep nginx filters the output to show only nginx processes.
feedback.bad: Pipe ps aux into grep: ps aux | grep nginx

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Show all running processes for all users.
steps.hint: Use ps with the aux flags — a=all users, u=user format, x=include processes without a terminal.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>USER       PID %CPU %MEM COMMAND
root         1  0.0  0.1 /sbin/init
user      1234  0.1  0.4 bash
user      1567  0.0  0.2 ssh-agent</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: ps aux (shows all processes from all users in a readable format)</info>

lessons.title: Real-time monitoring — top
lessons.description: Watch live CPU and memory usage with top

steps.label: top — live process viewer
steps.text:
<cmd>top</cmd> shows a live, updating view of all processes. It refreshes every 3 seconds by default.

Key controls inside top:
<hl>q</hl>        — quit
<hl>k</hl>        — kill a process (type its PID)
<hl>M</hl>        — sort by memory usage
<hl>P</hl>        — sort by CPU usage
<hl>1</hl>        — show individual CPU cores

<hl>htop</hl> is a friendlier version with colour and mouse support. Install it with <cmd>sudo apt install htop</cmd>.

The top header shows overall CPU%, memory, and load averages — three numbers for 1, 5, and 15 minute averages.

terminal.output:
top - 09:15:01 up 2 days,  3:22,  2 users,  load average: 0.12, 0.08, 0.05
Tasks: 142 total,   1 running, 141 sleeping
%Cpu(s):  2.1 us,  0.5 sy,  0.0 ni, 97.2 id
MiB Mem :   7832.0 total,   3211.0 free,   2890.0 used

  PID USER     %CPU %MEM COMMAND
 1234 user      1.0  0.8 bash
  890 root      0.3  0.2 sshd

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: Inside top, which key sorts processes by memory usage?

feedback.ok: Correct! M (capital) sorts by memory. P sorts by CPU.
feedback.bad: Press M (capital) to sort by memory usage, P to sort by CPU.

steps.label: Load average
steps.question: top shows load averages of 0.5, 1.2, 0.8. What do these three numbers represent?
options: Load averages over the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes
Number of processes, threads, and connections

feedback.ok: Correct! The three load averages are 1-minute, 5-minute, and 15-minute windows.
feedback.bad: Load averages show how busy the system has been: the three numbers are 1, 5, and 15 minute windows.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To quit top and return to the terminal, press

feedback.ok: Correct! q exits top cleanly. Ctrl+C also works.
feedback.bad: Press q to exit top gracefully. Ctrl+C also works in a pinch.

steps.label: htop vs top
steps.question: What is the main advantage of htop over top?
options: htop has colour output, mouse support, and easier navigation

feedback.ok: Correct! htop is more user-friendly with colour, mouse clicks, and easier key controls.
feedback.bad: htop improves on top with a colour interface, mouse support, and more intuitive controls. It may need to be installed.

lessons.title: Stopping processes — kill
lessons.description: Send signals to processes to stop, restart, or communicate with them

steps.label: Signals and kill
steps.text:
Linux communicates with processes through <hl>signals</hl>.

<cmd>kill PID</cmd>         — send SIGTERM (15) — asks the process to stop gracefully
<cmd>kill -9 PID</cmd>      — send SIGKILL — forces immediate termination, no cleanup
<cmd>kill -1 PID</cmd>      — send SIGHUP — reload config (useful for servers)
<cmd>killall nginx</cmd>    — kill all processes named 'nginx'
<cmd>pkill -f pattern</cmd> — kill processes matching a pattern

<warn>Use kill -9 as a last resort — the process gets no chance to save data or clean up.</warn>

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What is the difference between kill PID and kill -9 PID?
options: kill requests graceful shutdown; kill -9 forces immediate termination
kill PID only works as root

feedback.ok: Correct! SIGTERM (default) asks nicely. SIGKILL (-9) is forceful and immediate.
feedback.bad: kill sends SIGTERM — a polite request to stop. kill -9 sends SIGKILL — the process is terminated immediately with no chance to clean up.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To kill every process named 'python3' at once, use

feedback.ok: Correct! killall kills all processes with that exact name.
feedback.bad: killall sends a signal to all processes matching the given name: killall python3

steps.label: Which signal?
steps.question: An nginx server is running. You updated its config file and want it to reload without restarting. Which signal do you send?

feedback.ok: Correct! SIGHUP (kill -1) tells many server processes to reload their configuration.
feedback.bad: SIGHUP (-1) is the 'reload config' signal. Many servers like nginx and sshd respond to it by re-reading their config files.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Forcefully kill the process with PID 4821.
steps.hint: Use kill with the -9 flag followed by the PID.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: kill -9 4821
-9 sends SIGKILL, which cannot be caught or ignored by the process.</info>

lessons.title: Background & foreground jobs
lessons.description: Run commands in the background and switch jobs between foreground and background

steps.label: Job control
steps.text:
Linux lets you run multiple jobs in one terminal window.

<cmd>command &</cmd>       — start a command in the background
<cmd>Ctrl+Z</cmd>          — pause (suspend) the current foreground process
<cmd>jobs</cmd>            — list all background/paused jobs with their job number
<cmd>bg %1</cmd>           — resume job 1 in the background
<cmd>fg %1</cmd>           — bring job 1 back to the foreground

Job numbers (like %1, %2) are different from PIDs — they're just numbered per-terminal session.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You're running a long command. You press Ctrl+Z. What happens?
options: The command is cancelled and deleted
The command is paused and sent to the background in a stopped state

feedback.ok: Correct! Ctrl+Z suspends the process. You can then resume it with fg or bg.
feedback.bad: Ctrl+Z suspends (pauses) the process — it's still alive, just stopped. Use fg to bring it back or bg to resume it in the background.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To start a command running in the background immediately, add
to the end of the command.

feedback.ok: Correct! Appending & starts the process in the background: sleep 300 &
feedback.bad: Append & to run any command in the background: command &

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Bring background job number 2 back to the foreground:

feedback.ok: Correct! fg brings a job to the foreground. %2 specifies job number 2.
feedback.bad: fg %2 — the fg command followed by the job number. Job numbers are listed by the jobs command.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: List all current background and suspended jobs in this terminal session.
steps.hint: Use the jobs command. It shows all jobs with their number and status.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>[1]   Running    python3 server.py &
[2]-  Stopped    vim notes.txt
[3]+  Running    npm run watch &</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: jobs — it lists all jobs running or paused in the current shell session.</info>

lessons.title: Persistent processes — nohup
lessons.description: Keep processes running after you log out

steps.label: nohup and disown
steps.text:
When you close a terminal or log out via SSH, Linux sends a <hl>SIGHUP</hl> signal to all your processes, killing them.

<cmd>nohup command &</cmd>    — run a command that survives logout
  Output goes to <hl>nohup.out</hl> by default

<cmd>nohup command > out.log 2>&1 &</cmd>  — redirect output to a file

<cmd>disown %1</cmd>          — detach an already-running background job from the terminal (it won't get SIGHUP)

For long-running server processes, <hl>screen</hl> or <hl>tmux</hl> are even better — they let you reattach to the session later.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: Why does a background process die when you close your SSH session?
options: SSH kills all processes when disconnecting
The process runs out of memory
The terminal sends SIGHUP to all child processes on exit
Background processes always have a time limit

feedback.ok: Correct! Closing the terminal sends SIGHUP to all processes in that session.
feedback.bad: When a terminal closes, it sends SIGHUP to all its child processes. nohup and disown protect against this.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: By default, nohup saves the program's output to a file called

feedback.ok: Correct! nohup redirects all output to nohup.out in the current directory.
feedback.bad: nohup saves output to nohup.out by default. You can redirect it yourself: nohup cmd > myfile.log &

steps.label: nohup vs disown
steps.question: What is the key difference between nohup and disown?
options: nohup is used before starting; disown is used on an already-running job
disown keeps output; nohup discards it
nohup works only on root processes

feedback.ok: Correct! You wrap a command with nohup at start time, but you can disown a process that's already running.
feedback.bad: nohup must be used when starting a command. disown can be used on a job that's already running in the background.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Start backup.sh in the background so it keeps running after you log out, saving output to backup.log.
steps.hint: Use nohup, then the command, then redirect both stdout and stderr to backup.log, then &.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! backup.sh will keep running after logout.</success>
<info>[1] 5621</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: nohup bash backup.sh > backup.log 2>&1 &
2>&1 redirects stderr to the same file as stdout.</info>

lessons.title: Process priority — nice & renice
lessons.description: Control how much CPU a process gets relative to others

steps.label: nice values
steps.text:
Linux schedules CPU time between processes using a <hl>nice</hl> value.

Range: <hl>-20</hl> (highest priority) to <hl>+19</hl> (lowest priority). Default is 0.

Think of it as how 'nice' the process is to others — a high nice value means it gives up CPU time readily.

<cmd>nice -n 10 command</cmd>     — start a command at nice level 10 (lower priority)
<cmd>renice 15 -p 4521</cmd>      — change the nice value of running process 4521
<cmd>renice -5 -p 4521</cmd>      — raise priority (needs sudo if lowering nice below 0)

Use high nice values for background tasks (backups, compression) so they don't slow down interactive work.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: Which nice value gives a process the highest CPU priority?

feedback.ok: Correct! -20 is the highest priority. Lower nice = less 'nice' to other processes = more CPU.
feedback.bad: -20 is the highest priority. The scale runs from -20 (greedy, high priority) to +19 (very nice, low priority).

steps.label: Which tool?
steps.question: You started a backup job and it's making your computer slow. The job has PID 6200. How do you lower its priority without stopping it?

feedback.ok: Correct! renice changes the priority of an already-running process. 19 = very low priority.
feedback.bad: renice changes priority of a running process. nice is only for starting new processes. renice 19 -p 6200 gives the job very low priority.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To start a compression job at the lowest possible priority so it doesn't impact other tasks, run: nice -n

feedback.ok: Correct! nice -n 19 is the lowest priority — the process will only use CPU when nothing else needs it.
feedback.bad: +19 is the lowest priority (most 'nice' to others). -20 would be the highest priority.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Start the script sync_data.sh at a low priority (nice level 10) in the background.
steps.hint: Combine nice -n 10, the command, and & to background it.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! sync_data.sh started at nice level 10.</success>
<info>[1] 6740</info>

lessons.title: Section 5 review
lessons.description: Test your understanding of processes and job control

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You see a process with STAT 'S' in ps aux. What does this mean?
options: Sleeping — waiting for input or a resource

feedback.ok: Correct! S = sleeping. Most processes spend most of their time in this state.
feedback.bad: S means sleeping — the process is alive but waiting for something (disk, network, keyboard input).

steps.label: Review
steps.question: What command lists jobs running in your current terminal session?

feedback.ok: Correct! jobs shows background and suspended processes in your terminal session with job numbers.
feedback.bad: jobs lists all running and stopped jobs in the current shell session, showing job numbers like [1], [2].

steps.label: Review
sentence: To kill all processes named 'node' at once, use

feedback.ok: Correct! killall kills all processes with the given name.
feedback.bad: killall sends SIGTERM to all processes matching the name: killall node

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You start a task with nohup python3 job.py > out.log 2>&1 & and log out. What happens to the process?
options: It stops immediately when you log out
It keeps running — nohup protects it from SIGHUP
It pauses until you log back in
It runs once more then stops

feedback.ok: Correct! nohup makes the process ignore SIGHUP, so it keeps running after you disconnect.
feedback.bad: nohup makes the process immune to SIGHUP — the signal sent when you log out. The process keeps running.

steps.label: Review
steps.instruction: Start server.py in the background at low priority:

feedback.ok: Correct! nice -n 15 python3 server.py &
feedback.bad: Order: nice -n 15 (priority) then the command (python3 server.py) then & to background it.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: Which command would you use to bring background job [2] back to the foreground?

feedback.ok: Correct! fg %2 brings job 2 to the foreground.
feedback.bad: fg %jobnum brings a background job to the foreground. bg resumes a stopped job in the background.
